variant_label: Oil production & trade — Iraq oil reserves, proved
notes: Iraq's proved oil reserves increased this year to 100 billion barrels -- second in the world only to Saudi Arabia's. [cite: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.iii/1]

variant_label: Oil production & trade — Iraq projected oil exports by 1990
notes: Assuming oil prices of $13-16/barrel, Iraq could earn $15-20 billion in oil revenues annually by 1990 (highest since 1980) on exports of about 3.3 million b/d. [cite: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.iii]

variant_label: Oil production & trade — Iraq projected annual oil revenue by 1990
notes: Iraq could earn $15-20 billion in oil revenues annually by 1990 -- the highest level since 1980 -- given the assumed price/export scenario. [cite: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.iii]

variant_label: External debt — Iraq non-Arab military and civilian debt
notes: Servicing Iraq's $40 billion non-Arab military and civilian debt will probably be Baghdad's major postwar challenge. [cite: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.iii]

variant_label: Oil production & trade — Iran current oil production capacity
notes: Iran's current oil production capacity of roughly 3 million b/d is about half what it was 10 years ago (i.e. under the Shah). [cite: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.iii]

variant_label: External debt — Iraq non-Arab military/civilian debt rise
notes: Iraq's heavy borrowing caused its non-Arab military and civilian debt to rise from about $5 billion in 1979 to $40 billion in 1987, funding both military and civilian imports Tehran could not match. [cite: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.1]

variant_label: Foreign aid — Interest-free 'soft' loans to Iraq from Arab Gulf allies since 1980
notes: Iraq received $35 billion in interest-free 'soft' loans since 1980 from its Arab allies in the Persian Gulf (mainly Saudi Arabia and Kuwait), via Neutral Zone crude oil sales on Iraq's behalf and cash payments. [cite: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.1]

variant_label: Foreign exchange — Iraq foreign assets (reserves + gold) decline
notes: Iraq's foreign assets, including foreign exchange reserves and gold, fell from $36 billion in 1980 to about $3 billion in 1987. [cite: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.1]

variant_label: Trade — Iraq civilian imports level
notes: In 1987, Iraq's civilian imports amounted to $7.1 billion -- roughly half the 1980 level -- as Baghdad restricted civilian imports to essentials like food and medicine plus defense/oil-industry materials. [cite: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.1]

variant_label: Foreign exchange — Iran foreign assets decline
notes: Iran's foreign assets fell from about $19 billion in 1980 to $5.5 billion 'last year' (1987), of which only an estimated $1.5 billion are liquid reserves. [cite: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.1]

variant_label: Foreign exchange — Iran liquid reserves within total foreign assets
notes: Of Iran's $5.5 billion in foreign assets, only an estimated $1.5 billion are liquid reserves. [cite: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.1]

variant_label: Trade — Iran civilian imports level, 1986
notes: Iranian civilian imports in 1986 -- about $8 billion -- were nearly 35 percent less than in 1980 despite a near-20-percent increase in population over the period. [cite: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.1]

variant_label: Trade — Iran civilian import decline vs. 1980
notes: Iran's 1986 civilian imports were nearly 35 percent below the 1980 level despite population growth of nearly 20 percent over the same period. [cite: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.1]

variant_label: Trade — Oil exports -- Iran
notes: Table 1 comparison row 'Oil exports', Iran column. 1987 data except demographics (midyear 1988). [cite: CIA NESA 88-10067, Table 1 "How Do Iran-Iraq Economies Compare?" (1987 data; demographics midyear 1988), November 1988, PDF p.9]

variant_label: Trade — Oil exports -- Iraq
notes: Table 1 comparison row 'Oil exports', Iraq column. 1987 data except demographics (midyear 1988). [cite: CIA NESA 88-10067, Table 1 "How Do Iran-Iraq Economies Compare?" (1987 data; demographics midyear 1988), November 1988, PDF p.9]

variant_label: Trade — Nonoil exports -- Iran
notes: Table 1 comparison row 'Nonoil exports', Iran column. 1987 data except demographics (midyear 1988). [cite: CIA NESA 88-10067, Table 1 "How Do Iran-Iraq Economies Compare?" (1987 data; demographics midyear 1988), November 1988, PDF p.9]

unit: USD (mostly petroleum products, manufactured goods, sulfur)
variant_label: Trade — Nonoil exports -- Iraq
notes: Table 1 comparison row 'Nonoil exports', Iraq column. 1987 data except demographics (midyear 1988). [cite: CIA NESA 88-10067, Table 1 "How Do Iran-Iraq Economies Compare?" (1987 data; demographics midyear 1988), November 1988, PDF p.9]

variant_label: External debt — Foreign debt -- Iran
notes: Table 1 comparison row 'Foreign debt', Iran column. 1987 data except demographics (midyear 1988). [cite: CIA NESA 88-10067, Table 1 "How Do Iran-Iraq Economies Compare?" (1987 data; demographics midyear 1988), November 1988, PDF p.9]

variant_label: External debt — Foreign debt -- Iraq
notes: Table 1 comparison row 'Foreign debt', Iraq column. 1987 data except demographics (midyear 1988). [cite: CIA NESA 88-10067, Table 1 "How Do Iran-Iraq Economies Compare?" (1987 data; demographics midyear 1988), November 1988, PDF p.9]

variant_label: Foreign exchange — Foreign assets -- Iran
notes: Table 1 comparison row 'Foreign assets', Iran column. 1987 data except demographics (midyear 1988). [cite: CIA NESA 88-10067, Table 1 "How Do Iran-Iraq Economies Compare?" (1987 data; demographics midyear 1988), November 1988, PDF p.9]

variant_label: Foreign exchange — Foreign assets -- Iraq
notes: Table 1 comparison row 'Foreign assets', Iraq column. 1987 data except demographics (midyear 1988). [cite: CIA NESA 88-10067, Table 1 "How Do Iran-Iraq Economies Compare?" (1987 data; demographics midyear 1988), November 1988, PDF p.9]

variant_label: Balance of Payments (Net) — Estimated current account deficit -- Iran
notes: Table 1 comparison row 'Estimated current account deficit', Iran column. 1987 data except demographics (midyear 1988). [cite: CIA NESA 88-10067, Table 1 "How Do Iran-Iraq Economies Compare?" (1987 data; demographics midyear 1988), November 1988, PDF p.9]

variant_label: Balance of Payments (Net) — Estimated current account deficit -- Iraq
notes: Table 1 comparison row 'Estimated current account deficit', Iraq column. 1987 data except demographics (midyear 1988). [cite: CIA NESA 88-10067, Table 1 "How Do Iran-Iraq Economies Compare?" (1987 data; demographics midyear 1988), November 1988, PDF p.9]

variant_label: Trade — Crude oil share of Iran's hard-currency earnings
notes: Crude oil sales still account for at least 90 percent of Iran's hard currency earnings despite nonoil exports (carpets, textiles, pistachios) almost doubling their SHARE of total exports since 1985. [cite: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.5]

variant_label: Trade — Iran nonoil export earnings, annual
notes: Foreign sales of Iranian nonoil goods have earned almost a billion dollars annually in recent years. [cite: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.5]

variant_label: Trade — Oil export revenue share of Iraq's export earnings
notes: Iraq's oil export revenues account for about 95 percent of its export earnings. [cite: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.5]

variant_label: Oil production & trade — Iraq oil exports after Gulf terminal closures/Syria pipeline closure
notes: Closing of Iraq's Gulf terminals (1980) and Syria's closure of the Iraq-Syria pipeline (1982) slashed Iraqi oil exports to less than 1 million b/d. [cite: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.6]

variant_label: Oil production & trade — Iraq-Turkey pipeline capacity before 1984 expansion
notes: Iraq increased the capacity of its pipeline through Turkey from about 700,000 b/d to 1 million b/d in 1984. [cite: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.6]

variant_label: Oil production & trade — Iraq-Turkey pipeline capacity after 1984 expansion
notes: Iraq-Turkey pipeline capacity reached 1 million b/d in 1984. [cite: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.6]

variant_label: Oil production & trade — Iraq-Turkey pipeline capacity after further 1987 expansion
notes: The Iraq-Turkey pipeline line was expanded to 1.5 million b/d in 1987. [cite: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.6]

variant_label: Oil production & trade — Iraq-Saudi (Petroline) pipeline link to Yanbu, initial capacity
notes: Baghdad began exporting oil through the 500,000 b/d pipeline link to Saudi Arabia's Petroline to its Red Sea port at Yanbu al Bahr in 1985. [cite: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.6]

variant_label: Oil production & trade — Iraqi-Saudi pipeline capacity after phase-two expansion
notes: Phase two of the Iraqi-Saudi pipeline (construction begun 1987) will increase the line's capacity to 1.6 million b/d. [cite: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.6]

variant_label: Oil production & trade — Iraq total export capacity after pipeline expansions, near-prewar level
notes: The pipeline expansions will enable Iraq to export near-prewar levels of 3.5 million b/d. [cite: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.6]

variant_label: Oil production & trade — Iran oil production capacity decline vs. Shah-era peak
notes: Tehran's neglect resulted in a large reduction of oil production capacity from the roughly 6 million b/d reached under the Shah (to the ~3 million b/d cited in the Key Judgments). [cite: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.6]

variant_label: Oil production & trade — Abadan refinery capacity destroyed by Iraq early in the war
notes: Iraq destroyed Iran's 500,000 b/d refinery at Abadan early in the war. [cite: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.7]

variant_label: Oil production & trade — Iran forced refined-product imports due to refinery war damage
notes: Additional Iraqi strikes against Iran's remaining refineries have forced Iran to import roughly 200,000 b/d of refined products. [cite: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.7]

variant_label: Oil production & trade — Iraq oil export capacity when spot price dipped below $9/barrel
notes: Iraq's oil export capacity was 1.8 million b/d in 1986 when spot prices dipped below $9 per barrel. [cite: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.8]

variant_label: Oil production & trade — Iraq oil export capacity, mid-1988
notes: By mid-1988 Iraqi export capacity had risen to 2.3 million b/d. [cite: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.8]

variant_label: Oil production & trade — Iraq projected further export capacity rise from IPSA-2 pipeline
notes: Iraqi export capacity is expected to rise by another 1.1 million b/d in early 1990 when the IPSA-2 pipeline through Saudi Arabia is completed. [cite: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.8]

variant_label: Trade — Iran nonmilitary imports sourced from OECD
notes: Tehran relied on the OECD for about 70 percent of its nonmilitary imports in 1987, worth about $6.1 billion. [cite: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.11]

variant_label: Trade — Iran nonmilitary imports from OECD, dollar value
notes: Iran's OECD-sourced nonmilitary imports in 1987 were worth about $6.1 billion. [cite: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.11]

variant_label: Trade — West Germany/Japan sales to Iran, each
notes: West Germany and Japan are by far Iran's two largest suppliers, each selling at least $1 billion worth of goods to Iran in 1987. [cite: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.11]

variant_label: Trade — US exports to Iran
notes: US exports to Iran in 1987 totaled just $60 million -- compared with $4 billion in 1978. [cite: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.11]

variant_label: Trade — US exports to Iran, 1978 (pre-revolution baseline)
notes: US exports to Iran were $4 billion in 1978, the pre-revolution baseline against which the 1987 collapse to $60 million is measured. [cite: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.11]

variant_label: Trade — US imports from Iran
notes: The US imported more goods from Iran in 1987 -- $1.6 billion -- than any other country, despite the US trade embargo against Iranian goods enacted in late 1987 (after which Iranian exports to the US ceased). [cite: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.11]

variant_label: Trade — Iran exports to OECD
notes: Iran exported $7.1 billion worth of goods (mostly oil) to the OECD in 1987, equivalent to roughly 60 percent of its sales abroad. [cite: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.11]

variant_label: Trade — Iran exports to US and Japan combined, share of OECD exports
notes: Iran's exports to the United States and Japan alone represented at least 35 percent of its $7.1 billion in OECD exports. [cite: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.11]

variant_label: Foreign exchange — Financing sources, total
notes: Table 2 line item 'Financing sources, total' -- projected Iraq financing sources/requirements for 1988. Financing gap of -$5.7bn is sources minus requirements. [cite: CIA NESA 88-10067, Table 2 "Iraq: Financing Sources and Requirements, 1988" (projected), November 1988, PDF p.12]

variant_label: Foreign exchange — Financing sources -- oil export revenues
notes: Table 2 line item 'Financing sources -- oil export revenues' -- projected Iraq financing sources/requirements for 1988. Financing gap of -$5.7bn is sources minus requirements. [cite: CIA NESA 88-10067, Table 2 "Iraq: Financing Sources and Requirements, 1988" (projected), November 1988, PDF p.12]

variant_label: Foreign exchange — Financing sources -- nonoil export revenues
notes: Table 2 line item 'Financing sources -- nonoil export revenues' -- projected Iraq financing sources/requirements for 1988. Financing gap of -$5.7bn is sources minus requirements. [cite: CIA NESA 88-10067, Table 2 "Iraq: Financing Sources and Requirements, 1988" (projected), November 1988, PDF p.12]

variant_label: Foreign aid — Financing sources -- Arab aid
notes: Table 2 line item 'Financing sources -- Arab aid' -- projected Iraq financing sources/requirements for 1988. Financing gap of -$5.7bn is sources minus requirements. [cite: CIA NESA 88-10067, Table 2 "Iraq: Financing Sources and Requirements, 1988" (projected), November 1988, PDF p.12]

variant_label: Foreign exchange — Financial requirements, total
notes: Table 2 line item 'Financial requirements, total' -- projected Iraq financing sources/requirements for 1988. Financing gap of -$5.7bn is sources minus requirements. [cite: CIA NESA 88-10067, Table 2 "Iraq: Financing Sources and Requirements, 1988" (projected), November 1988, PDF p.12]

variant_label: Trade — Financial requirements -- civilian imports
notes: Table 2 line item 'Financial requirements -- civilian imports' -- projected Iraq financing sources/requirements for 1988. Financing gap of -$5.7bn is sources minus requirements. variant_label: Balance of Payments (misc) — Financial requirements -- invisibles

variant_label: External debt — Financial requirements -- principal payments on foreign debt

variant_label: External debt — Financial requirements -- interest payments on foreign debt
notes: Table 2 line item 'Financial requirements -- interest payments on foreign debt' -- projected Iraq financing sources/requirements for 1988. variant_label: Foreign exchange — Financing gap
notes: Table 2 line item 'Financing gap' -- projected Iraq financing sources/requirements for 1988. Financing gap of -$5.7bn is sources minus requirements. [cite: CIA NESA 88-10067, Table 2 "Iraq: Financing Sources and Requirements, 1988" (projected), November 1988, PDF p.12]

variant_label: Trade — Iraq civilian imports total, 1987
notes: Iraq's civilian import purchases were about $3.8 billion in 1987, smaller than Iran's despite Iraq's heavier Western dependence. [cite: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.12]

variant_label: Trade — Iraq civilian imports sourced from OECD, decade average share
notes: During the 1980s, Iraq purchased an average of 70 percent of its civilian imports from OECD countries. variant_label: External debt — Iraq debt to Western governments/banks/firms
notes: By yearend 1987, Iraq had accumulated nearly $30 billion in debt to Western governments, commercial banks, and private firms for trade and project financing. [cite: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.13]

variant_label: Trade — Iraq exports delivered to OECD share
notes: Iraq delivers about 55 percent of its exports (mostly oil) to the OECD; main customers Japan, Italy, West Germany. [cite: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.13]

notes: Figure 9 pie chart, Iran side: United Kingdom supplied 6% of Iran's 1987 civilian imports. [cite: CIA NESA 88-10067, Figure 9 "Iran-Iraq: Sources of Civilian Imports, 1987" (percent, pie chart with printed values), November 1988, PDF p.21]

notes: Figure 9 pie chart, Iran side: LDCs supplied 18% of Iran's 1987 civilian imports. [cite: CIA NESA 88-10067, Figure 9 "Iran-Iraq: Sources of Civilian Imports, 1987" (percent, pie chart with printed values), November 1988, PDF p.21]

variant_label: Trade — Iraq civilian imports source share -- Japan

variant_label: Trade — Iraq civilian imports source share -- France
notes: Figure 9 pie chart, Iraq side: France supplied 6% of Iraq's 1987 civilian imports. [cite: CIA NESA 88-10067, Figure 9 "Iran-Iraq: Sources of Civilian Imports, 1987" (percent, pie chart with printed values), November 1988, PDF p.21]

variant_label: Trade — Iraq civilian imports source share -- United Kingdom
notes: Figure 9 pie chart, Iraq side: United Kingdom supplied 7% of Iraq's 1987 civilian imports. [cite: CIA NESA 88-10067, Figure 9 "Iran-Iraq: Sources of Civilian Imports, 1987" (percent, pie chart with printed values), November 1988, PDF p.21]

variant_label: Trade — Iraq civilian imports source share -- West Germany
notes: Figure 9 pie chart, Iraq side: West Germany supplied 7% of Iraq's 1987 civilian imports. [cite: CIA NESA 88-10067, Figure 9 "Iran-Iraq: Sources of Civilian Imports, 1987" (percent, pie chart with printed values), November 1988, PDF p.21]

variant_label: Trade — Iraq civilian imports source share -- Soviet Bloc
notes: Figure 9 pie chart, Iraq side: Soviet Bloc supplied 9% of Iraq's 1987 civilian imports. [cite: CIA NESA 88-10067, Figure 9 "Iran-Iraq: Sources of Civilian Imports, 1987" (percent, pie chart with printed values), November 1988, PDF p.21]

variant_label: Trade — Iraq civilian imports source share -- United States
notes: Figure 9 pie chart, Iraq side: United States supplied 11% of Iraq's 1987 civilian imports. [cite: CIA NESA 88-10067, Figure 9 "Iran-Iraq: Sources of Civilian Imports, 1987" (percent, pie chart with printed values), November 1988, PDF p.21]

variant_label: Trade — Iraq civilian imports source share -- Turkey
notes: Figure 9 pie chart, Iraq side: Turkey supplied 15% of Iraq's 1987 civilian imports. [cite: CIA NESA 88-10067, Figure 9 "Iran-Iraq: Sources of Civilian Imports, 1987" (percent, pie chart with printed values), November 1988, PDF p.21]

variant_label: Trade — Iraq civilian imports source share -- Other OECD
notes: Figure 9 pie chart, Iraq side: Other OECD supplied 16% of Iraq's 1987 civilian imports. [cite: CIA NESA 88-10067, Figure 9 "Iran-Iraq: Sources of Civilian Imports, 1987" (percent, pie chart with printed values), November 1988, PDF p.21]

variant_label: Trade — Iraq civilian imports source share -- LDCs
notes: Figure 9 pie chart, Iraq side: LDCs supplied 23% of Iraq's 1987 civilian imports. [cite: CIA NESA 88-10067, Figure 9 "Iran-Iraq: Sources of Civilian Imports, 1987" (percent, pie chart with printed values), November 1988, PDF p.21]

variant_label: External debt — Iraq debt to the Soviet Bloc
notes: Iraq's debt to the Bloc -- mostly to the USSR for military purchases -- was about $7.5 billion at yearend 1987. [cite: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.16]

variant_label: Foreign aid — Soviet-claimed involvement in Iraq development projects
notes: In December 1987 the USSR claimed to be involved in nearly 100 projects in Iraq, per the US Embassy in Baghdad, though many are believed on hold due to Iraq's financial constraints. [cite: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.16]

variant_label: Trade — Soviet Bloc share of Iraq civilian imports
notes: The Bloc has supplied about 10 percent of Iraq's civilian imports in the 1980s. [cite: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.16]

variant_label: Trade — East European (esp. Romania/Bulgaria) share of Bloc civilian sales to Iraq
notes: East European countries -- especially Romania and Bulgaria -- have made about 90 percent of Bloc sales of civilian goods to Iraq (vs. the USSR dominating military sales). [cite: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.16]

variant_label: External debt — Iraq foreign military+civilian debt by source, total (yearend 1987, excl. $35bn Gulf Arab soft debt)
notes: Figure 13 pie chart total: $40 billion in Iraqi foreign military and civilian debt at yearend 1987, EXCLUDING $35 billion in soft debt owed to Gulf Arab countries. [cite: CIA NESA 88-10067, Figure 13 "Iraq: Foreign Military and Civilian Debt by Source, Yearend 1987", November 1988, PDF p.23]

variant_label: External debt — Iraq debt-by-source share -- Soviet Bloc
notes: Figure 13 pie chart: Soviet Bloc accounts for 12% of Iraq's $40 billion foreign military/civilian debt at yearend 1987 (West subtotal = government 37 + commercial banks 20 + private firms 18 = 75%). [cite: CIA NESA 88-10067, Figure 13 "Iraq: Foreign Military and Civilian Debt by Source, Yearend 1987" (percent of $40bn total, excl. $35bn Gulf Arab soft debt), November 1988, PDF p.23]

variant_label: External debt — Iraq debt-by-source share -- Third World
notes: Figure 13 pie chart: Third World accounts for 13% of Iraq's $40 billion foreign military/civilian debt at yearend 1987 (West subtotal = government 37 + commercial banks 20 + private firms 18 = 75%). [cite: CIA NESA 88-10067, Figure 13 "Iraq: Foreign Military and Civilian Debt by Source, Yearend 1987" (percent of $40bn total, excl. $35bn Gulf Arab soft debt), November 1988, PDF p.23]

variant_label: External debt — Iraq debt-by-source share -- West -- government
notes: Figure 13 pie chart: West -- government accounts for 37% of Iraq's $40 billion foreign military/civilian debt at yearend 1987 (West subtotal = government 37 + commercial banks 20 + private firms 18 = 75%). [cite: CIA NESA 88-10067, Figure 13 "Iraq: Foreign Military and Civilian Debt by Source, Yearend 1987" (percent of $40bn total, excl. $35bn Gulf Arab soft debt), November 1988, PDF p.23]

variant_label: External debt — Iraq debt-by-source share -- West -- commercial banks
notes: Figure 13 pie chart: West -- commercial banks accounts for 20% of Iraq's $40 billion foreign military/civilian debt at yearend 1987 (West subtotal = government 37 + commercial banks 20 + private firms 18 = 75%). [cite: CIA NESA 88-10067, Figure 13 "Iraq: Foreign Military and Civilian Debt by Source, Yearend 1987" (percent of $40bn total, excl. $35bn Gulf Arab soft debt), November 1988, PDF p.23]

variant_label: External debt — Iraq debt-by-source share -- West -- private firms
notes: Figure 13 pie chart: West -- private firms accounts for 18% of Iraq's $40 billion foreign military/civilian debt at yearend 1987 (West subtotal = government 37 + commercial banks 20 + private firms 18 = 75%). [cite: CIA NESA 88-10067, Figure 13 "Iraq: Foreign Military and Civilian Debt by Source, Yearend 1987" (percent of $40bn total, excl. $35bn Gulf Arab soft debt), November 1988, PDF p.23]

variant_label: Trade — Soviet imports of Iranian natural gas, 1970s
notes: In the 1970s, Moscow imported some 10 billion cubic meters per year of Iranian natural gas, before price disputes virtually terminated the sales. [cite: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.16]

variant_label: Trade — Iran-Soviet trade, annual value
notes: Iranian-Soviet trade remained small -- about $200 million annually -- compared with Iran's Western trade, despite 1987 meetings to improve ties. [cite: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.16]

variant_label: Foreign aid — Cost estimate for USSR-Persian Gulf rail link or new Iran gas export pipeline
notes: Larger schemes such as a USSR-to-Persian-Gulf rail link or renewed Iranian gas exports (requiring a new pipeline) would cost at least $1 billion each -- an amount Iran cannot, and Moscow will not, provide. [cite: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.17]

variant_label: Trade — Iran trade with Eastern Europe, annual value
notes: Iranian trade with Eastern Europe amounted to about $1 billion in 1987 -- about 5 percent of Iran's total trade. [cite: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.17]

variant_label: Trade — Eastern Europe share of Iran's total trade
notes: Iran-East Europe trade represented about 5 percent of Iran's total trade in 1987. [cite: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.17]

variant_label: Oil production & trade — Iraq projected annual oil revenue by 1990 (Key Judgments restated in outlook section)
notes: Restated in the outlook section: Iraq's oil revenues could reach $15-20 billion annually by 1990 because of increased oil export capacity. [cite: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.19]

variant_label: External debt — Iraq annual principal + interest payments due, next five years
notes: Iraq faces $6 billion in principal and interest payments due each year for at least the next five years -- Baghdad probably will not institute severe austerity to free up resources for this. [cite: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.19]

variant_label: Oil production & trade — Iran investment needed to reverse oil-industry erosion since the revolution
notes: It could easily take $15-30 billion to begin reversing the erosion in Iran's oil industry that has occurred since the revolution. [cite: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.19]
